pageTimings.text: Deep underground, rock gets so hot that it melts. This melted rock is called magma. Magma is hotter than anything you can imagine.
Sometimes, magma pushes up through a crack in the Earth. When it bursts out at the surface, we call it a volcano! The melted rock that flows out is called lava.
Lava can be as hot as 1,000 degrees. Some volcanoes are calm and slow. Their lava oozes out like very thick, very hot syrup.
Other volcanoes are very explosive. They blast ash, smoke and rocks high into the sky. The blast can be heard hundreds of kilometres away.
Volcanoes can be found on land and under the sea. When an underwater volcano keeps erupting, the lava builds up and can make a brand new island!
After a volcano, the cooled lava becomes new rock. Volcanic rock breaks down into rich, dark soil. Many plants and crops grow very well in volcanic soil.
Famous volcanoes can be found all around the world. Iceland, Hawaii and Italy all have active volcanoes. Indonesia has more volcanoes than any other country.
Most volcanoes are quiet for a very long time. Scientists called volcanologists watch them carefully. They use special equipment to spot the signs of an eruption.
Volcanoes can seem frightening. But they have helped shape our planet. They make new land, rich soil and even new islands. They have been doing this for billions of years.
